example_prompt: Receive a charge.completed webhook from Flutterwave, verify the HMAC-SHA256 signature, then fulfil the customer order.

type.description: Event type. Always "charge.completed" for this webhook.

id.description: Unique webhook event id (e.g. "wbk_W5p6ktwU0jQ8RO4By860"). Use it to deduplicate retried deliveries. NOTE: some Flutterwave v4 webhook payloads use "webhook_id" instead of "id" — handle both.

webhook_id.description: Alternative top-level event id field — Flutterwave is inconsistent across event types (refund.completed uses webhook_id, charge.completed historically uses id). Treat as a fallback for id.

timestamp.description: Unix epoch timestamp in milliseconds when the event was emitted.

id.description: Charge id. Re-fetch via GET /charges/{id} to verify server-side.

payment_method.description: Snapshot of the payment method used.

response_schema.description: Your endpoint must respond with HTTP 200 and an (optionally empty) JSON body within 60 seconds.

error_schema.description: Webhooks do not return errors to Flutterwave. Any non-2xx response triggers retries.

gotchas: Flutterwave signs webhook payloads with HMAC-SHA256 using the secret hash configured in your dashboard. Compute HMAC-SHA256 of the raw request body and compare with the 'flutterwave-signature' header. Reject mismatches. Respond with HTTP 200 within 60 seconds — Flutterwave retries failed webhooks 3 times at 30-minute intervals.

Webhook payloads contain a top-level 'type' field (e.g. 'charge.completed', 'refund.completed') and a 'data' object with the resource details. Always verify the transaction by calling GET /charges/{id} server-side before fulfilling — never trust webhook data alone.
Use the raw request body (bytes/string) when computing HMAC — re-serialising parsed JSON will reorder keys and break verification.
Use a constant-time comparison (e.g. node:crypto timingSafeEqual) when comparing signatures to avoid timing-side-channel attacks.
Deduplicate on the top-level event id — Flutterwave may redeliver the same event after a transient failure on your side.
data.status is not always 'succeeded' on a charge.completed event. Check the status field explicitly before fulfilling; failed/voided charges also generate this event.
